refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) remains at or below the customer-defined E_target throughout the evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across the range of supported worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 is a high-level KPI requirement that must be decomposed into measurable, controllable functional requirements. The refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is a system-level outcome dependent on multiple subsystems (propulsion, hydraulics, teleoperation overhead, idle management, load optimization). The requirement now explicitly bounds the evaluation context (nominal conditions, evaluation period, worksite scenarios) to enable testability and allocation.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry collection of energy consumption (kWh or fuel equivalent) and work output (tons moved) across representative worksite missions; post-evaluation statistical analysis to confirm energy_per_ton ≤ E_target with confidence interval; disaggregation by mission type and operator mode (autonomous vs. tele-operated) to identify efficiency drivers and bottlenecks.
sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The propulsion subsystem shall minimize energy loss during load movement.
FR-EE-002: The hydraulic subsystem shall minimize parasitic energy consumption during idle and partial-load states.
FR-EE-003: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize network and processing overhead energy per unit of remote control command.
FR-EE-004: The vehicle control system shall optimize load path and duty cycle to reduce wasted motion and energy dissipation.
FR-EE-005: The system shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility to operators to enable behavioral efficiency optimization.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a closed-loop energy management system with real-time monitoring, predictive load modeling, and adaptive control of propulsion and hydraulic subsystems to minimize energy_per_ton across all operating modes.
DP-B: Specify high-efficiency drivetrain components (motor, transmission, hydraulic pump) and enforce idle-state power-down policies; rely on operator training and worksite procedures for efficiency.

DP-C: Decompose energy efficiency into subsystem-level targets (propulsion efficiency ≥ 92%, hydraulic parasitic loss ≤ 8%, teleoperation overhead ≤ 5% of total energy) and allocate design responsibility to each subsystem owner with independent verification.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Decompose energy efficiency into subsystem-level targets (propulsion efficiency ≥ 92%, hydraulic parasitic loss ≤ 8%, teleoperation overhead ≤ 5% of total energy) and allocate design responsibility to each subsystem owner with independent verification.

potential_sources: Propulsion and hydraulic subsystems are coupled via load-sharing and duty-cycle optimization; independent targets may conflict if not coordinated through a system-level energy budget.
Teleoperation overhead is coupled to network latency and command frequency; allocation must account for variable network conditions.
Operator behavior (load path, idle time) is a major efficiency driver but is not directly controlled by design; behavioral targets must be separated from technical design targets.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C is preferred because it enables parallel subsystem development and clear accountability while maintaining system-level traceability. Coupling risks are manageable through explicit energy budget allocation and integration testing. DP-A (closed-loop optimization) is more ambitious but introduces complexity and validation risk; DP-B (component selection alone) is insufficient to guarantee system-level KPI achievement without operational controls.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Subsystem-level efficiency targets (92%, 8%, 5%) are achievable with current technology and cost constraints; risk: targets may be infeasible or require expensive components.
Assumption: Operator behavior (load path, idle management) can be influenced through training and real-time feedback; risk: actual operator behavior may deviate significantly from design assumptions.
Assumption: Energy_per_ton can be accurately measured and attributed to subsystems; risk: measurement uncertainty or data quality issues may obscure true efficiency performance.
Assumption: Evaluation period and worksite scenarios are representative of customer's actual usage; risk: field performance may differ from controlled evaluation conditions.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly include a system-level energy budget allocation process that accounts for coupling between subsystems and operator behavior. Add a feedback loop requirement: 'The system shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility and efficiency metrics to operators and maintenance teams to enable continuous optimization.' This bridges the gap between technical design targets and operational efficiency.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary that integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. At this level, the IM is responsible for energy efficiency KPI achievement through subsystem coordination, energy budget allocation, telemetry aggregation, and system-level performance monitoring.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Energy consumption data (kWh, fuel equivalent), work output (tons moved), efficiency metrics (energy_per_ton), subsystem performance indicators, and operational context (mission type, operator mode, worksite conditions) are transmitted to external monitoring and analytics systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Mission objectives, load specifications, worksite constraints, and efficiency targets are received from the Site Manager or planning system to inform energy optimization decisions.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety-critical energy management decisions (e.g., power-down during emergency, reduced-speed mode) are coordinated with Safety Services; alerts regarding energy system faults or efficiency anomalies are reported.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communication with Teleoperation subsystem for remote control commands and feedback; network latency and bandwidth constraints impact teleoperation energy overhead.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level container for energy efficiency KPI. All subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) contribute to energy_per_ton. Containment is high because energy efficiency is a system-level emergent property that requires integration and coordination across subsystems. Minimal diffusion: energy budget allocation and telemetry aggregation are localized to NGHE Solution level.

Vehicle subsystem contains propulsion, hydraulics, and load-handling mechanisms that directly impact energy consumption. However, energy efficiency KPI is not solely a Vehicle responsibility; Teleoperation overhead and Safety Services constraints also contribute. If assigned to Vehicle alone, energy budget allocation and system-level coordination would diffuse to external interfaces, reducing cohesion.

Teleoperation subsystem is responsible for remote control energy overhead, but energy_per_ton is a system-level metric that depends on Vehicle propulsion efficiency and operational context. Assigning the KPI to Teleoperation alone would require diffuse dependencies on Vehicle and Safety Services, violating cohesion.

Safety Services may impose constraints on energy efficiency (e.g., reduced-speed mode, power-down requirements) but does not directly control energy consumption. Assigning the KPI to Safety Services would create high diffusion and unclear ownership.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Energy efficiency KPI (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is a system-level emergent property that arises from the coordinated operation of Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. The NGHE Solution IM is the appropriate container because it owns the energy budget allocation process, telemetry aggregation, and system-level performance monitoring. Subsystem-level targets (propulsion efficiency, hydraulic parasitic loss, teleoperation overhead) are allocated downward to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services respectively, with NGHE Solution responsible for integration and verification.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution IM may become a 'catch-all' for cross-cutting concerns if subsystem boundaries are not clearly defined. Mitigation: Establish explicit energy budget allocation and interface contracts between NGHE Solution and subsystems.

Risk: Operator behavior (load path, idle management) is a major efficiency driver but is not directly controlled by any IM. Mitigation: Allocate a sub-FR to Vehicle or Safety Services for 'operator guidance and feedback' to influence behavior within design assumptions.

Risk: Telemetry interface may become a bottleneck if energy data collection is not efficiently designed. Mitigation: Define Telemetry interface contract to specify data granularity, frequency, and compression to minimize overhead.

Risk: Coupling between Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems (network latency impacts control responsiveness and energy efficiency) may not be visible at NGHE Solution level. Mitigation: Include network performance metrics in Telemetry interface and establish feedback loops for adaptive control.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine the NGHE Solution IM scope to explicitly include: (1) Energy budget allocation and tracking across subsystems, (2) Telemetry aggregation and real-time efficiency monitoring, (3) Coordination of energy-related constraints from Safety Services, (4) Integration testing and verification of energy_per_ton KPI. Define clear interface contracts for Telemetry (data schema, frequency, latency), Tasking (efficiency target communication), and SafetyAlert (energy-safety coordination). Establish a System Energy Manager module within NGHE Solution to own energy budget allocation and optimization logic.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR clearly states the energy efficiency KPI (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) with explicit bounds on evaluation context. DP-C (subsystem-level target allocation) directly addresses the FR by decomposing the system-level KPI into measurable subsystem targets and establishing accountability. The mapping is traceable: each subsystem target contributes to the overall energy_per_ton metric. Coupling risks are identified and manageable through energy budget allocation and integration testing.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Energy budget allocation logic may diffuse across multiple subsystems if not centralized. Mitigation: Establish a System Energy Manager module within NGHE Solution to own allocation and coordination.

Risk: Telemetry aggregation and real-time monitoring may be distributed across subsystems, reducing visibility. Mitigation: Define Telemetry interface contract to specify data schema and aggregation responsibility.

Risk: Operator behavior feedback loops may be unclear if responsibility is not explicitly assigned. Mitigation: Allocate a sub-FR to Vehicle or Safety Services for 'operator guidance' with clear success criteria.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution IM is the appropriate container for DP-C because it owns system-level energy budget allocation, telemetry aggregation, and KPI verification. Subsystem-level targets are allocated downward to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, with clear interface contracts. Containment score of 95 indicates high cohesion and minimal diffusion. External interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and traceable to DP-C.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Energy_per_ton can be verified through continuous telemetry collection and post-evaluation statistical analysis. Subsystem-level targets (propulsion efficiency, hydraulic parasitic loss, teleoperation overhead) can be verified through component-level testing and integration testing. Operator behavior can be monitored through telemetry and worksite observations. Verification is feasible but requires robust data collection, quality assurance, and analysis processes.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The refined FR is allocated to NGHE Solution IM with clear subsystem-level targets (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-005) assigned to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Each subsystem target is measurable and traceable to the system-level KPI. Allocation is clear and enables parallel subsystem development with system-level integration verification.

recommended_revisions: Refine the energy budget allocation process to account for coupling between subsystems and operator behavior. Establish explicit energy targets for each subsystem with confidence intervals and sensitivity analysis.
Define the Telemetry interface contract to specify energy data schema, collection frequency, latency, and aggregation responsibility. Ensure data quality and traceability for KPI verification.

Establish a feedback loop requirement: 'The system shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility and efficiency metrics to operators and maintenance teams to enable continuous optimization.' This bridges technical design targets and operational efficiency.
Allocate a sub-FR to Safety Services for 'energy-safety coordination' to ensure that safety-critical constraints (e.g., reduced-speed mode, power-down) are accounted for in energy budget allocation.

Define success criteria for operator behavior: 'Operators shall achieve ≥ 80% of theoretical maximum efficiency through training and real-time feedback.' This separates technical design targets from behavioral targets.

decision.justification: The refined FR (CR-NGHE-003) is decomposed into five sub-FRs (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-005) that address propulsion, hydraulics, teleoperation, vehicle control, and operator feedback. DP-C (subsystem-level target allocation) is selected and assigned to NGHE Solution IM with clear external interfaces. The FR-DP-IM mapping is traceable and passes all gate reviews. However, the sub-FRs are not yet atomic and require further decomposition to enable detailed design and implementation. Proceeding to the next level will refine subsystem-level targets and establish detailed design parameters for Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems.
decision.justification_detail: ZIGZAG_DOWN is appropriate because: (1) The current level (NGHE Solution) is the correct architectural container for the energy efficiency KPI, (2) DP-C provides a sound decomposition strategy (subsystem-level targets), (3) Sub-FRs are well-defined but require further refinement to be atomic, (4) The next level will allocate sub-FRs to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems with detailed design parameters and implementation modules.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The propulsion subsystem shall minimize energy loss during load movement (target: ≥ 92% efficiency).
FR-EE-002: The hydraulic subsystem shall minimize parasitic energy consumption during idle and partial-load states (target: ≤ 8% of total energy).
FR-EE-003: The teleoperation subsystem shall minimize network and processing overhead energy per unit of remote control command (target: ≤ 5% of total energy).
FR-EE-004: The vehicle control system shall optimize load path and duty cycle to reduce wasted motion and energy dissipation.
FR-EE-005: The system shall provide real-time energy consumption visibility to operators to enable behavioral efficiency optimization.

assumptions: Subsystem-level efficiency targets (92%, 8%, 5%) are achievable with current technology and cost constraints.
Operator behavior can be influenced through training and real-time feedback to achieve ≥ 80% of theoretical maximum efficiency.
Energy_per_ton can be accurately measured and attributed to subsystems with acceptable measurement uncertainty (< 5%).
Evaluation period and worksite scenarios are representative of customer's actual usage patterns.
Network latency and bandwidth constraints for Teleoperation are within acceptable bounds to achieve 5% overhead target.
Safety Services constraints (e.g., reduced-speed mode, power-down) can be accommodated within energy budget allocation without exceeding E_target.
open_risks: Risk: Subsystem-level targets may be infeasible or require expensive components; mitigation: conduct feasibility study and cost-benefit analysis during detailed design.
Risk: Operator behavior may deviate significantly from design assumptions; mitigation: implement real-time feedback and adaptive control to guide operators toward efficient operation.
Risk: Measurement uncertainty or data quality issues may obscure true efficiency performance; mitigation: establish robust data collection, validation, and analysis processes.
Risk: Field performance may differ from controlled evaluation conditions; mitigation: conduct extended field trials and continuous monitoring to validate KPI achievement.
Risk: Coupling between subsystems may create unexpected efficiency losses; mitigation: conduct integration testing and sensitivity analysis to identify and mitigate coupling effects.
Risk: Teleoperation overhead may exceed 5% target due to network latency or command frequency; mitigation: optimize command protocol and implement adaptive control to minimize overhead.
evidence_needed: Feasibility study: Confirm that subsystem-level efficiency targets (92%, 8%, 5%) are achievable with current technology and cost constraints.
Operator behavior analysis: Characterize typical operator behavior (load path, idle time, command frequency) and identify opportunities for efficiency improvement through training and feedback.
Measurement system design: Define energy data collection, validation, and aggregation processes to ensure data quality and traceability for KPI verification.
Network performance analysis: Characterize network latency, bandwidth, and reliability under typical worksite conditions to validate teleoperation overhead target.
Integration testing plan: Define test scenarios and success criteria for verifying energy_per_ton KPI across subsystem combinations and operating modes.
Field trial plan: Define evaluation period, worksite scenarios, and data collection procedures for validating KPI achievement in customer's actual usage environment.
